cards.text: A {{c1::friend function}} is a non-member function that has been granted access to the {{c2::private and protected}} members of a class.
To declare a friend function, use the {{c1::friend}} keyword inside the class: {{c1::friend}} ReturnType functionName();
Friend functions are {{c1::not member functions}}, so they {{c2::do not have a this pointer}} and are not called using the {{c3::dot operator}}.
Friend functions are commonly used for {{c1::operator overloading}} (like {{c2::operator<<}}) where the class object is not the {{c3::left operand}}.
The friend keyword can appear in any {{c1::access specifier section}} (public, private, protected) - the placement {{c2::doesn't matter}}.

cards.front: What is the difference between a friend function and a member function?
cards.back:
Friend functions:
  • Are not member functions
  • Have no this pointer
  • Are called like regular functions, not with dot operator
  • But can access private members

cards.front: Why use friend functions instead of member functions for operator<< overloading?
cards.back: Because std::cout << obj requires cout to be the left operand, which means the function cannot be a member of your class. It needs to be a non-member (friend) function.

cards.question: Complete the friend function to print a Point.
cards.answer: Inside class: friend void print(const Point& p);
cards.explanation: Use friend keyword to grant the non-member function access to private members m_x and m_y.

cards.question: How do you declare operator<< as a friend for this class?
cards.answer: friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Value& v);
cards.explanation: Declare as friend so it can access private m_value. Returns ostream& for chaining.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Friend functions are not member functions, so they cannot be called using the dot operator.

cards.explanation:
Friend functions are appropriate when:
  • Overloading operators where the class object isn't the left operand (like operator<<)
  • A function needs access to private data of multiple classes
  • A function is conceptually part of the class interface but can't be a member
  • Use sparingly - they break encapsulation
examples: friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const MyClass& obj);
friend bool areEqual(const MyClass& a, const MyClass& b);

cards.back: A non-member function that has been granted access to the private and protected members of a class
